commentary: Psychological safety is not about being nice. It is about being able to say the hard thing without it costing you your standing. The nicest teams I've studied are often the least safe, because everyone is protecting the peace instead of naming the problem.

New episode of Leading Out Loud is up. This week I talk with a hospital ops leader about what it takes to build a genuine speak-up culture when the stakes are literally life and death. Her line stuck with me: silence is the most expensive thing on the floor.

A manager told me his team had no conflict. I asked how many people had disagreed with him in the last month. He couldn't name one. That's not harmony. That's a team that has learned it isn't safe to push back.

Writing update on The Safety Equation: I spent this week rewriting the chapter on measurement. If you can't measure psychological safety, executives treat it as a mood. If you can, it becomes a lever. The whole book rests on making the invisible countable.

Three questions I ask every leadership team before we start work: When did someone last tell you bad news early? What happened to them afterward? Would they do it again? The answers tell me more than any engagement survey.

Trust and psychological safety are not the same thing. Trust is my belief about you over time. Safety is what the group makes possible in the moment. You can trust a colleague deeply and still not feel safe raising a concern in the room. Design for both.

The fastest way to kill candor is to punish the first honest person. Leaders think they are correcting a comment. The team reads it as a warning. What gets modeled at the top gets mirrored everywhere below.

Brock Leadership Group is booking a limited number of leadership team engagements for the winter. Our work centers on turning psychological safety from a slogan into a set of practices your managers can actually run. Reach out through brockleadershipgroup.com.

On this week's Leading Out Loud, a biotech people leader and I dig into how scientific teams handle being wrong. Turns out the labs that publish the most are also the ones most comfortable saying the experiment failed. Being wrong out loud is a competitive advantage.

The research is clear and a little uncomfortable: teams don't underperform because people lack skill. They underperform because people hold back what they know. Capability sitting in silence is the most common waste in the modern workplace.

End of year reflection. Twenty years in and the thing I'm most sure of is the simplest: people do their best work when they aren't managing fear. Everything else is technique. Grateful to the leaders who let me into their teams this year.

Kicking off the year with a question I keep returning to: what would your team say if they knew there'd be no consequence? If the honest version of that answer surprises you, that gap is where your real work is.

Every organization says it wants feedback. Fewer notice that they've quietly built a system that rewards agreement. Look at who gets promoted after a tough meeting. That's your real feedback policy, not the one on the wall.

New Leading Out Loud episode: a manufacturing L&D leader on building trust across a shop floor where the last three change programs failed. Her approach was refreshingly unglamorous. Show up, keep one promise at a time, repeat. Culture is compounding reliability.

A note on humor at work, since I get asked. Dry humor lowers the stakes just enough for people to say the true thing. Sarcasm aimed at a person does the opposite. The difference matters more than most leaders think.

The Safety Equation is now in the hands of my first outside readers. Handing over a draft is its own exercise in psychological safety. I'd be a hypocrite to ask for honest feedback and then flinch at it. So far the flinch reflex is strong and the feedback is better for it.

Reshared with a note. Brian Mitchell's new paper on speak-up behavior in high-reliability settings is worth your time. His finding that proximity to leadership predicts silence more than seniority does is going to reshape how I frame a lot of my work.

Assessments like Hogan and DiSC are maps, not verdicts. I use them to start a conversation, never to end one. The moment a team treats a personality type as an excuse, the tool has stopped helping and started hiding.

On Leading Out Loud this week: a VP of People on the unglamorous mechanics of psychological safety at scale. Her point that safety is not a value you announce but a habit you enforce in a thousand small moments is the whole ballgame.

A leader asked me to make his team more resilient. I asked what he wanted them to survive. Half the time resilience is code for asking people to absorb a problem the system should have fixed. Sometimes the kindest intervention is to remove the thing they keep bouncing back from.

Manuscript milestone: I finished the chapter on how silence spreads. It moves the way any norm moves, one person watching what happened to the last person who spoke. Write that dynamic down and it stops feeling mysterious and starts feeling fixable.

For the folks new to my feed: I'm an organizational psychologist who helps leaders build teams where candor is the norm, not the exception. If your team gets quiet exactly when it matters most, that's the problem I work on. Say hello, I read the comments.
